contracts.notes: block-derived randomness is a real secondary issue even though the official label only counts the uninitialized-storage bug
unchecked arithmetic and push-payment failure behavior are both defensible secondary issues
ignored send() results and payout-loop griefing are real secondary issues beyond the official wrong-constructor label
the contract repeatedly ignores send() return values in payout and refund paths, which is a real secondary unchecked-call issue beyond the array-reset DOS bug
resetInvestment() ignores the return values of two send() operations, which is a real secondary unchecked-call issue beyond the timestamp-dependent game reset
the wallet contains real unchecked low-level interaction risk beyond the public reinitialization path counted by the official benchmark
the public DranMe() function is a wrong-constructor-name takeover that lets any caller seize owner privileges beyond the official unchecked-call labels
endRaffle() performs transfer() inside a refund loop, so one reverting recipient can block the whole admin refund path, which is a real secondary DOS issue beyond the benchmark's randomness labels
the contract exposes public initialization functions SetMinSum, SetLogFile, and Initialized without an owner gate, which is a real secondary access-control issue beyond the official reentrancy label
PopBonusCode() performs a real unsigned length underflow via bonusCodes.length--, which is a source-real arithmetic issue beyond the official access-control label
the contract exposes public initialization functions SetMinSum, SetLogFile, and Initialized without an owner gate, which is a real secondary access-control issue beyond the official reentrancy label

initTokenBank() is a public owner-reset path that lets any caller seize privileged control before draining token or Ether holders, so the extra access-control finding is real
the onlyOwner modifier uses tx.origin == owner, which is a real authorization flaw beyond the official reentrancy label
the contract exposes public initialization functions SetMinSum, SetLogFile, and Initialized without an owner gate, which is a real secondary access-control issue beyond the official reentrancy label

channel close routines perform transfer() to counterparties during settlement, so one reverting recipient can block payout finalization and create a real secondary DOS condition
withdraw() performs msg.sender.call.value(...)() before zeroing balances[msg.sender], so the file contains a real reentrancy issue in addition to the official unchecked-call label
withdrawLeftOver() is publicly callable after payedOut flips and lets any caller drain the remaining contract balance, which is a real secondary access-control failure
